sections.label: Section 1068
sections.original: κᾆτʼ ἀπολιποῦσά γʼ αὐτὸν ᾤχετʼ · οὐ γὰρ ἦν ὑβριστὴς
sections.translation: And then she went off and left him ; for he was not lustful , nor an agreeable bedfellow to spend the night with .

sections.label: Section 1070
sections.original: γυνὴ δὲ σιναμωρουμένη χαίρει · σὺ δʼ εἶ Κρόνιππος .
sections.translation: Now a woman delights in being wantonly treated . But you are an old dotard . For ( to Phidippides ). consider , O youth , all that attaches to modesty , and of how many pleasures you are about to be deprived — of women , of games at cottabus , of dainties , of drinking-bouts , of giggling . And yet , what is life worth to you if you be deprived of these enjoyments ?

sections.label: Section 1075
sections.original: εἶεν . πάρειμʼ ἐντεῦθεν ἐς τὰς τῆς φύσεως ἀνάγκας .
sections.translation: Well , I will pass from thence to the necessities of our nature . You have gone astray , you have fallen in love , you have been guilty of some adultery , and then have been caught . You are undone , for you are unable to speak . But if you associate with me , indulge your inclination , dance , laugh , and think nothing disgraceful . For if you should happen to be detected as an adulterer , you will make this reply to him ,

sections.label: Section 1080
sections.original: ὡς οὐδὲν ἠδίκηκας · εἶτʼ ἐς τὸν Δίʼ ἐπανενεγκεῖν ,
sections.translation: that you have done him no injury : and then refer him to Jupiter , how even he is overcome by love and women . And yet , how could you , who are a mortal , have greater power than a god ?

sections.label: Section 1083
sections.original: τί δʼ ἢν ῥαφανιδωθῇ πιθόμενός σοι τέφρᾳ τε τιλθῇ ,
sections.translation: But what if he should suffer the radish through obeying you , and be depillated with hot ashes ? What argument will he be able to state , to prove that he is not a blackguard ?

sections.label: Section 1085
sections.original: ἢν δʼ εὐρύπρωκτος ᾖ , τί πείσεται κακόν ;
sections.translation: And if he be a blackguard , what harm will he suffer ?

sections.label: Section 1086
sections.original: τί μὲν οὖν ἂν ἔτι μεῖζον πάθοι τούτου ποτέ ;
sections.translation: Nay , what could he ever suffer still greater than this ?

sections.label: Section 1087
sections.original: τί δῆτʼ ἐρεῖς , ἢν τοῦτο νικηθῇς ἐμοῦ ;
sections.translation: What then will you say if you be conquered by me in this ?

sections.label: Section 1088
sections.translation: I will be silent : what else can I do ?

sections.label: Section 1088b
sections.translation: Come , now , tell me ; from what class do the advocates come ?

sections.label: Section 1090
sections.translation: From the blackguards .

sections.label: Section 1090b
sections.translation: I believe you . What then ? From what class do tragedians come ?

sections.label: Section 1092
sections.translation: From the blackguards .

sections.label: Section 1092b
sections.translation: You say well . But from what class do the public orators come ?

sections.label: Section 1094
sections.translation: From the blackguards .

sections.label: Section 1094b
sections.translation: Then have you perceived that you say nothing to the purpose ? And look which class among the audience is the more numerous .

sections.label: Section 1097b
sections.translation: Well now , I ’ m looking .

sections.label: Section 1098
sections.translation: What , then , do you see ?

sections.label: Section 1098a
sections.translation: By the gods , the blackguards to be far more numerous . This fellow , at any rate , I know ; and him yonder ; and this fellow with the long hair .

sections.label: Section 1101a
sections.translation: What , then , will you say ?

sections.label: Section 1102
sections.translation: We are conquered . Ye blackguards , by the gods , receive my cloak , for I desert to you .

sections.label: Section 1105
sections.original: τί δῆτα ; πότερα τοῦτον ἀπάγεσθαι λαβὼν
sections.translation: What then ? whether do you wish to take and lead away this your son , or shall I teach him to speak ?

sections.label: Section 1107
sections.original: δίδασκε καὶ κόλαζε καὶ μέμνησʼ ὅπως
sections.translation: Teach him , and chastise him : and remember that you train him properly ; on the one side able for petty suits ; but train his other jaw able for the more important causes .

sections.label: Section 1111
sections.translation: Make yourself easy ; you shall receive him back a clever sophist .

sections.label: Section 1112
sections.original: ὠχρὸν μὲν οὖν οἶμαί γε καὶ κακοδαίμονα .
sections.translation: Nay , rather , pale and wretched .

sections.label: Section 1113
sections.original: χωρεῖτέ νυν . οἶμαι δέ σοι ταῦτα μεταμελήσειν .
sections.translation: Go ye , then : but I think that you will repent of these proceedings .

sections.label: Section 1115
sections.original: τοὺς κριτὰς ἃ κερδανοῦσιν , ἤν τι τόνδε τὸν χορὸν
sections.translation: We wish to speak about the judges , what they will gain , if at all they justly assist this Chorus . For in the first place , if you wish to plough up your fields in spring , we will rain for you first ; but for the others afterward . And then we will protect the fruits , and the vines ,

sections.label: Section 1120
sections.original: ὥστε μήτʼ αὐχμὸν πιέζειν μήτʼ ἄγαν ἐπομβρίαν .
sections.translation: so that neither drought afflict them , nor excessive wet weather . But if any mortal dishonour us who are goddesses , let him consider what evils he will suffer at our hands , obtaining neither wine nor anything else from his farm . For when his olives and vines sprout , they shall be cut down ; with such slings

sections.label: Section 1125
sections.translation: will we smite them . And if we see him making brick , we will rain ; and we will smash the tiles of his roof with round hailstones . And if he himself , or any one of his kindred or friends , at any time marry , we will rain the whole night ; so he will probably wish rather to have been

sections.label: Section 1130
sections.original: κἂν ἐν Αἰγύπτῳ τυχεῖν ὢν μᾶλλον ἢ κρῖναι κακῶς .
sections.translation: even in Egypt than to have judged badly .

sections.label: Section 1131
sections.original: πέμπτη , τετράς , τρίτη , μετὰ ταύτην δευτέρα ,
sections.translation: The fifth , the fourth , the third , after this the second ; and then , of all the days I most fear , and dread , and abominate , immediately after this there is the Old and New .

sections.label: Section 1135
sections.original: πᾶς γάρ τις ὀμνὺς οἷς ὀφείλων τυγχάνω ,
sections.translation: For every one to whom I happen to be indebted , swears , and says he will ruin and destroy me , having made his deposits against me ; though I only ask what is moderate and just — My good sir , one part don ’ t take just now ; the other part put off I pray ; and the other part remit ; they say that thus

sections.label: Section 1140
sections.translation: they will never get back their money , but abuse me , as I am unjust , and say they will go to law with me . Now therefore let them go to law , for it little concerns me , if Phidippides has learned to speak well . I shall soon know by knocking at the thinking-shop .

sections.label: Section 1145
sections.translation: Boy , I say ! Boy , boy !

sections.label: Section 1145b
sections.translation: Good morning , Strepsiades .

sections.label: Section 1146
sections.original: κἄγωγέ σʼ · ἀλλὰ τουτονὶ πρῶτον λαβέ ·
sections.translation: The same to you . But first accept this present ; for one ought to compliment the teacher with a fee . And tell me about my son , if he has learned that cause , which you just now brought forward .

sections.label: Section 1150
sections.translation: He has learned it .

sections.label: Section 1150b
sections.translation: Well done , O Fraud , all-powerful queen !

sections.label: Section 1151
sections.original: ὥστʼ ἀποφύγοις ἂν ἥντινʼ ἂν βούλῃ δίκην .
sections.translation: So that you can get clear off from whatever suit you please .

sections.label: Section 1152
sections.translation: Even if witnesses were present when I borrowed the money ?

sections.label: Section 1153
sections.original: πολλῷ γε μᾶλλον , κἂν παρῶσι χίλιοι .
sections.translation: Yea , much more ! Even if a thousand be present .

sections.label: Section 1154
sections.translation: Then I will shout with a very loud

sections.label: Section 1155
sections.translation: shout : Ho ! Weep , you petty-usurers , both you and your principals , and your compound interests ! For you can no longer do me any harm , because such a son is being reared for me in this house ,

sections.label: Section 1160
sections.translation: shining with a double-edged tongue , for my guardian , the preserver of my house , a mischief to my enemies , ending the sadness of the great woes of his father . Him do thou run and summon from within to me .

sections.label: Section 1165
sections.original: ὦ τέκνον ὦ παῖ ἔξελθʼ οἴκων ,
sections.translation: [*] O child ! O son ! Come forth from the house ! Hear your father ! Re-enter Socrates leading in Phidippides . Lo , here is the man !

sections.label: Section 1168
sections.translation: O my dear , my dear !

sections.label: Section 1169
sections.translation: Take your son and depart .

sections.label: Section 1170
sections.original: ἰὼ ἰὼ τέκνον , ἰὼ ἰοῦ ἰοῦ .
sections.translation: Oh , oh , my child ! Huzza ! Huzza ! How I am delighted at the first sight of your complexion ! Now , indeed , you are , in the first place , negative and disputatious to look at , and this fashion native to the place plainly appears , the what do you say ? and the seeming

sections.label: Section 1175
sections.original: ἀδικοῦντʼ ἀδικεῖσθαι καὶ κακουργοῦντʼ οἶδʼ ὅτι ·
sections.translation: to be injured when , I well know , you are injuring and inflicting a wrong ; and in your countenance there is the Attic look . Now , therefore , see that you save me , since you have also ruined me .

sections.label: Section 1178
sections.translation: What , pray , do you fear ?

sections.label: Section 1178b
sections.translation: The Old and New .

sections.label: Section 1179
sections.original: ἕνη γάρ ἐστι καὶ νέα τις ἡμέρα ;
sections.translation: Why , is any day old and new ?

sections.label: Section 1180
sections.original: εἰς ἥν γε θήσειν τὰ πρυτανεῖά φασί μοι .
sections.translation: Yes ; on which they say that they will make their deposits against me .

sections.label: Section 1181
sections.original: ἀπολοῦσʼ ἄρʼ αὔθʼ οἱ θέντες · οὐ γὰρ ἔσθʼ ὅπως
sections.translation: Then those that have made them will lose them ; for it is not possible that two days can be one day .

sections.label: Section 1183
sections.translation: Can not it ?

sections.label: Section 1183b
sections.original: πῶς γάρ ; εἰ μή πέρ γʼ ἅμα
sections.translation: Certainly not ; unless the same woman can be both old and young at the same time .

sections.label: Section 1185
sections.translation: And yet it is the law .

sections.label: Section 1185b
sections.translation: For they do not , I think , rightly understand what the law means .

sections.label: Section 1186b
sections.translation: And what does it mean ?

sections.label: Section 1187
sections.original: ὁ Σόλων ὁ παλαιὸς ἦν φιλόδημος τὴν φύσιν .
sections.translation: The ancient Solon was by nature the commons ’ friend .
